findings.message: Command file has no YAML frontmatter block; `name` field is absent so NLPM cannot register or discover this command
Command file has no YAML frontmatter block; `description` field is absent so NLPM scoring and auto-classification fail
Command declares no `allowed-tools` field; tools used (Bash, ExitWorktree) are undeclared, making automated tool-permission audits unreliable
`$ARGUMENTS` is embedded in the command but no guard handles the case where it is empty; the Pre-flight section proceeds unconditionally
"Stage relevant files by name" — "relevant" is undefined; no criterion distinguishes relevant from irrelevant files
Command file has no YAML frontmatter block; `name` field is absent so NLPM cannot register or discover this command
Command file has no YAML frontmatter block; `description` field is absent so NLPM scoring and auto-classification fail
Command declares no `allowed-tools` field; tools used to read @-referenced docs are undeclared
`$ARGUMENTS` is echoed at the end with no empty-input guard; if called with no arguments the final instruction `Then process the user's request: ` is a dangling imperative
State file at `/tmp/claude-stop-${SESSION_ID}.state` uses SESSION_ID from untrusted JSON hook input; predictable path enables TOCTOU / symlink attacks in shared `/tmp/`

`printf '%b'` in MSG construction interprets backslash escapes from `$FILE_LIST`, which contains filenames from `git diff` output; crafted filenames with `\n` or `\t` can corrupt the JSON reason string sent to Claude Code

`$file` (from hook JSON `attached_files[]`) is interpolated directly into a double-quoted echo forming a JSON object; a filename containing `"` or `\` produces malformed JSON and could inject a `"decision":"allow"` field, silently bypassing the security block

`$file_path` originates from JSON `attached_files[]` and is passed to `stat` and `cat` without verifying the path remains within the project root; attacker-influenced input could read arbitrary host files

Baseline file at `/tmp/claude-baseline-${SESSION_ID}.numstat` uses SESSION_ID from untrusted hook JSON; a pre-created symlink or file at that path can poison the git-diff baseline, causing the stop hook to under-count new changes
Comment on L6 documents intended usage as `curl -fsSL … | bash` — executes arbitrary remote code without inspection; compromised GitHub repo yields RCE on all users running the one-liner
Script downloads a tarball from GitHub (L90) and executes the extracted `setup.sh` directly (L188) without signature or checksum verification — functionally identical to curl-pipe-bash in two steps

All shell command blocks contain `[YOUR_TEST_COMMAND]`, `[YOUR_DEPLOY_COMMAND]`, etc. — skill is structurally correct but non-functional until customised; a naive invocation would produce no useful output
Workflow ends at `Step 5 — Verify` (read the output image) with no explicit confirmation message to the user; no summary of what was done or where the output file lives
Workflow ends at Step 6 `Post-process` (copy + delete) with no formal report to the user; final state and chosen variant path are not confirmed
"If none are good enough" (L104) and "Pick the single best variant" (L102) use subjective criteria with no measurable threshold for acceptance
"relevant source files changed" (L29) and "substantial implementation work" (description) use undefined thresholds

Multiple vague intensifiers without definition: "genuinely useful" (L11), "routine tasks" (L22), "real consequences" (L22), "add value" (L17) — each leaves the usage trigger undefined and model-dependent
Skill ends at Step 5 (create missing files) with no summary to the user of which files were updated and what changed
"Only update files where the change is meaningful" — "meaningful" is undefined; the model must guess what meets the bar

Sections 2 (Architecture), 4 (Coding Standards), 5 (Testing), and 6 (Privacy/Security) are entirely commented-out HTML comments with no active rules; a project that installs this template gains no guidance in those four areas
